title: Emit fix-guidance on a violation, not just the failing edge
description:
hex-lint currently reports a violation as a clean statement of the forbidden edge (consumer X may not depend on Y). That tells you what broke but not how to resolve it. The failure mode this opens: an AI agent under a 'make the build pass' objective will bounce from one guard rail to the next, padding an exception or rewiring blindly, burning tokens and time — exactly the problem Nick Tune calls out in 'Deterministic Code Conventions with Oxlint'.

His mitigation is a roleDefinitionsDir: a markdown file per role describing how to use it properly, including common misclassifications ('this is NOT a cli-entrypoint: entrypoints translate external input into a use-case input and call it; they do not load aggregates'). The agent reads these before writing code and re-reads them when it hits a guard rail, so it recovers fast instead of thrashing.

WHAT we want: a hex-lint violation should carry an actionable recovery channel — enough for a human or agent to fix the *architecture* (move the crate's role, extract a port, route through the composition root), not just learn that it's broken. The shape is open: per-role definition docs, a remediation hint attached to each matrix rule, a pointer the diagnostic prints, a 'hex-lint explain <role>' subcommand — pick what fits the tool.

Provenance: KBE source ~/kbe/sources/2026-06-22-tune-deterministic-code-conventions.md (the 'why this is here' section lists this as improvement #1 to lift from Tune). Note hex-lint already beats Tune on the honesty axis (stale exceptions fail the lint) — keep that; this is the missing complementary half.
close_reason: Added Role::remediation() (pure domain) emitting the broken rule + concrete fixes per consumer role. Wired inline into text output, attached as a 'remediation' object on every JSON violation (agent recovery channel), and exposed via 'hex-lint explain <role>'. Chose baked-in static advice keyed on role pair over Tune's user-maintained roleDefinitionsDir — fits hex-lint's 'matrix is hardcoded' stance, zero new IO, stays framework-free.

title: Decide and document hex-lint's stance on intra-crate (sub-crate) coupling
description:
hex-lint enforces role boundaries at Cargo workspace-member (crate) granularity: it tags each crate with a role and walks the workspace dependency graph, failing any cross-role edge the matrix forbids. That makes intra-crate violations invisible — a 'domain' module importing 'infra' *inside one crate*, or a use-case reaching into an adapter within the same crate, passes the lint clean. Tune's Oxlint role-enforcement went file/decorator-level precisely because crate/package edges miss this.

This is the real granularity gap between hex-lint and the file-level tools (dependency-cruiser, Oxlint role-enforcement), and it's a scope/architecture decision, not a quick patch. WHAT we want: a deliberate, documented stance — either (a) hex-lint owns sub-crate granularity (module/file-level role tags + intra-crate edge checks), or (b) it explicitly scopes itself to crate boundaries and points users at clippy / a sibling tool for the inside-a-crate story. Either answer is fine; what's not fine is leaving the guarantee ambiguous, because a user who tags crates and sees 'clean' will assume their layering is enforced when intra-crate violations slipped through.

Whatever the decision, it belongs in the README so the guarantee hex-lint makes is unambiguous.

Provenance: KBE source ~/kbe/sources/2026-06-22-tune-deterministic-code-conventions.md (improvement #2 from Tune); article https://nick-tune.me/blog/2026-04-10-deterministic-code-conventions-oxlint/
close_reason: Decision: (b) hex-lint is scoped to CRATE granularity, deliberately — owning sub-crate enforcement needs the module/AST graph (a syn-based rewrite), and the crate boundary is the only boundary Rust actually enforces. Documented in README (new 'Scope: crate granularity' section making the guarantee unambiguous) and in --help (SCOPE block).

title: Add orthogonal context-isolation check
description:
Add a second, orthogonal check to hex-lint: context isolation. The hardcoded role matrix proves dependency arrows respect layer direction but is blind to bounded contexts — e.g. a driving-adapter in context 'shopping' may legally (per matrix) depend on a usecase in context 'pantry', which is forbidden per architecture. Close that gap.

Rule (single total predicate over the same workspace-internal edge set): an edge consumer->dep passes iff consumer.context == dep.context OR dep.context == 'shared'. This subsumes 'shared may only depend on shared'. 'shared' is the one reserved context name; others are free-form.

Opt-in: zero declared contexts => behaviour byte-identical to today. Partial adoption (some but not all members declare context) is a hard, run-aborting error, mirroring the existing untagged-role rule (fail closed). Role and context are independent checks — run both, report both, keep logic in separate files so either can be deleted without touching the other.

Exceptions stay one file/one schema with a new axis = 'role' | 'context' field defaulting to 'role' (backward compatible); suppression and stale-detection become per (consumer, dep, axis). Violation output names the axis, both crates, both contexts, and the rule. README documents the rule, the reserved 'shared' name, the precise soundness/completeness claim (full cargo-metadata graph, both predicates total, no approximation), and the named limitation (crate-dependency level only). Full acceptance criteria and design in plan: ~/.claude/plans/you-are-working-on-zesty-hellman.md

title: Restructure into neutral use-case core + per-axis scaffolding
description: Step-0 pre-work refactor, committed separately before the feature. Make the two-axis split structural, not just conventional. Move the neutral Remediation struct out of role.rs into its own domain file so both axes can share it without either depending on the other. Extract the role check (currently lint::run + Violation + LintReport) into its own use-case file. Slim lint.rs to the axis-agnostic core: shared DTOs, a generic per-axis report type, and a generic exception-reconciliation helper (unsanctioned + stale set arithmetic) that knows nothing about roles or contexts. Add an Axis enum and an axis field (default role, backward compatible) to the Exception DTO. WHY: the context check must stand on the same shared plumbing the role check does, and each axis must live in its own file. Keep all 39 existing tests green (relocate the role-check tests with the code).

title: Context domain: isolation predicate + adoption tri-state
description: New domain entity for the context axis, pure (no IO), and NOT importing the role domain. It owns: the reserved 'shared' name; the total edge predicate (pass iff same context OR dep is shared); the workspace-level adoption tri-state (disabled when zero members declare context, enabled when all do, partial — a hard error — when some do, naming the crates that lack it); and context-specific remediation guidance mirroring the role remediation. WHY: this is the context axis's business logic, isolated so it can be deleted without touching role logic.

title: Adapter intake: read optional context key from metadata
description: Extend the cargo-metadata adapter to read the optional context = '<name>' key from the same [package.metadata.hex-arch] table and carry it on each workspace package. Policy-free intake only — the adoption/partial rule lives in the context domain, not here. WHY: the context check needs each member's declared context; parsing is shared adapter plumbing, not check logic.

title: Context use case: compute context-isolation report
description: New use-case file for the context axis, mirroring the role check. Produces a per-axis report (violations, unsanctioned, stale) by applying the context predicate to every workspace-internal edge, skipping edges whose endpoint has no context (so it is naturally empty when context is disabled). Filters exceptions to the context axis before reconciling, so suppression and stale detection are per (consumer, dep, axis). WHY: the context verdict, kept in its own file and standing on the shared reconcile helper.

title: Wire both axes into the composition root
description: In main: resolve context adoption right after loading the workspace and abort with a hard error naming the offending crates on partial adoption (mirroring the untagged-role abort). Run both checks, fold both into the exit verdict. Extend text output with a context violation block (naming axis, both crates, both contexts, the rule + fixes) and a context-clean summary line that appears only when context is enabled — the role summary line stays byte-identical to today. Extend JSON additively: context violations/stale arrays, a third rule entry, axis fields, optional package context. WHY: run both, report both, exit non-zero on either; keep the opt-in invisible when unused.

title: Fixtures + integration harness + regression golden
description: Add self-contained fixture workspaces under tests/ (one per acceptance item), each a virtual workspace of empty path-dep member crates with role (+optional context) tags and a committed lockfile. Add a process-level integration harness that runs the compiled binary against each fixture via --manifest-path (this also gives the cargo-metadata adapter its first coverage) plus inline unit tests for the pure context predicate/adoption/check. Regression-lock the no-context case against a checked-in golden authored from the pre-change binary. Cover: clean same-context, the load-bearing cross-context edge (role passes / context fails, axis named), shared-dep OK, shared-reaches-in fails, partial-adoption hard error, context exception suppresses exactly its edge (and a role-axis exception does NOT), stale context exception fails. WHY: prove every EARS requirement and lock the opt-in regression.

title: Document context isolation in README + exception schema
description: Add a README section (same blunt voice as 'The matrix'/'Scope') stating: the rule; the single reserved 'shared' name; opt-in with partial adoption a hard error; exceptions share the file/schema with axis='context' (default role) and stale entries fail loud; what a green run certifies (full cargo-metadata graph, both predicates total, no approximation — a mislabeled context is the user's bug, not the tool's; claim no more); and the named limitation that isolation is enforced at the crate-dependency level only (a copy-pasted foreign type inside a crate is out of scope). Update the exceptions-file example and adapter doc-comment to show the optional axis key. WHY: the check is only done when discoverable and its guarantees are stated honestly.

title: Enforce the new test suite in CI
description: CI currently runs fmt/clippy/build/self-lint but not tests, so the new integration suite would be unguarded. Add a cargo test step. WHY: an unenforced test suite rots.
